---
format:
  revealjs:
    title: "Week 8 Lecture"
    subtitle: "Dr Shunya Kimura <br> skimura@liverpool.ac.uk"
    slide-number: true
    slide-level: 3
    output-file: "w8-slides-lecture.html"
    theme: ../scss/custom.scss
    revealjs-plugins:
      - pointer
    echo: true
---

### Sports Analytics in Practice {.not-bold}

#### API & Data Sourcing

![](/images/UoL%20Zoom%20Backgrounds_20226.jpg){.stretch fig-align="center"}

### Module Outline

```{=html}
<div style="width:100%; font-family:Poppins,sans-serif; font-size:0.98em; margin-top:2em;">

    <div style="display:flex; padding:0.35em 0; border-bottom:1px solid #ddd; color:#555;">
      <span style="width:3.2em; font-weight:600; color:#aaa;">W7</span>
      <span>Foundations of Reproducible Analytics</span>
    </div>
    <div style="display:flex; padding:0.35em 0; border-bottom:1px solid #ddd; color:#222;">
      <span style="width:3.2em; font-weight:700; color:#EF426F;">W8</span>
      <span style="font-weight:600;">API &amp; Data Sourcing</span>
    </div>
    <div style="display:flex; padding:0.35em 0; border-bottom:1px solid #ddd; color:#555;">
      <span style="width:3.2em; font-weight:600; color:#aaa;">W9</span>
      <span>Interactive Dashboards with Shinylive I</span>
    </div>
    <div style="display:flex; padding:0.35em 0; border-bottom:1px solid #ddd; color:#555;">
      <span style="width:3.2em; font-weight:600; color:#aaa;">W10</span>
      <span>Interactive Dashboards with Shinylive II</span>
    </div>
    <div style="display:flex; padding:0.35em 0; color:#555;">
      <span style="width:3.2em; font-weight:600; color:#aaa;">W11</span>
      <span>The Professional Portfolio</span>
    </div>

</div>
```

### Last Week

-   Installed the package stack for this block.
-   Created an RStudio project with a clean folder structure.
-   Connected the project to GitHub.
-   Made and pushed a first commit.

## Quick Recap

### Go To Workbook...

![](/images/workbook-recap.png){style="box-shadow: 5px 5px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.5);"}

::: notes
Go through recap and setting up the shortcut ./\_publish.sh
:::

## This Week

### Our Plan

-   Understand what an API is and why it matters.
-   Use the `hoopR` package to pull NBA data.
-   Inspect, clean and summarise raw data.
-   Save prepared datasets for later weeks.

## What Is an API?

### The Short Answer

-   **API** stands for **Application Programming Interface**.
-   It is a structured way for one piece of software to request data from another.
-   Instead of downloading a file manually, your R script asks a server to send the data directly.

### Why Analysts Care

-   Manual downloads are **not reproducible**. You cannot re-run them automatically.
-   APIs give you a **programmatic, repeatable** path to fresh data.
-   Most modern sports data platforms expose their data through APIs.

### The Mental Model

```{=html}
<div style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; gap:0.3em; margin-top:1em; font-size:1.05em;">

  <div style="padding:0.5em 2em; background:#264653; color:white; border-radius:8px; font-weight:600; text-align:center; width:70%;">
    1. Your R script calls a function
  </div>

  <div style="font-size:1.5em; color:#888;">↓</div>

  <div style="padding:0.5em 2em; background:#2a9d8f; color:white; border-radius:8px; font-weight:600; text-align:center; width:70%;">
    2. The function sends an API request
  </div>

  <div style="font-size:1.5em; color:#888;">↓</div>

  <div style="padding:0.5em 2em; background:#e76f51; color:white; border-radius:8px; font-weight:600; text-align:center; width:70%;">
    3. The server returns the data
  </div>

  <div style="font-size:1.5em; color:#888;">↓</div>

  <div style="padding:0.5em 2em; background:#EF426F; color:white; border-radius:8px; font-weight:600; text-align:center; width:70%;">
    4. You get a data frame in R
  </div>

</div>
```

### Everyday Analogies

-   A weather app on your phone uses an API to get forecast data.
-   When you search for flights, the site queries airline APIs behind the scenes.
-   APIs are just **structured conversations** between machines.

## SportsDataVerse

### What Is It?

#### <https://r.sportsdataverse.org/>

-   **SportsDataVerse** is a collection of open-source R and Python packages for accessing sports data.
-   Each package wraps an API for a specific sport or data source.
-   The API calls are hidden behind simple R functions.

### Key Packages

![R Packages in the SportDataverse](/images/sdv-wall.png){style="box-shadow: 5px 5px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.5);" fig-align="left"}

```{=html}
<div style="width:100%; font-family:Poppins,sans-serif; font-size:0.95em; margin-top:1.5em;">

    <div style="display:flex; padding:0.35em 0; border-bottom:1px solid #ddd; color:#222;">
      <span style="width:9em; font-weight:700; color:#EF426F;">hoopR</span>
      <span>NBA and men's college basketball</span>
    </div>
    <div style="display:flex; padding:0.35em 0; border-bottom:1px solid #ddd; color:#555;">
      <span style="width:9em; font-weight:600; color:#aaa;">wehoop</span>
      <span>WNBA and women's college basketball</span>
    </div>
    <div style="display:flex; padding:0.35em 0; border-bottom:1px solid #ddd; color:#555;">
      <span style="width:9em; font-weight:600; color:#aaa;">nflverse</span>
      <span>NFL play-by-play and roster data</span>
    </div>
    <div style="display:flex; padding:0.35em 0; border-bottom:1px solid #ddd; color:#555;">
      <span style="width:9em; font-weight:600; color:#aaa;">baseballr</span>
      <span>MLB Statcast and FanGraphs data</span>
    </div>
    <div style="display:flex; padding:0.35em 0; color:#555;">
      <span style="width:9em; font-weight:600; color:#aaa;">worldfootballR</span>
      <span>Football (soccer) data from FBref, Transfermarkt, etc.</span>
    </div>

</div>
```

### Why hoopR?

#### <https://hoopr.sportsdataverse.org/>

-   Rich, publicly available NBA data with no authentication required.
-   Clean R functions that return ready-to-use data frames.
-   The workflow pattern transfers to any sport — only the package and column names change.

## Reading the Documentation

### The Documentation Habit

-   Before you call a function, **read its documentation**.
-   The documentation tells you:
    -   what data sources are accessible;
    -   what **functions** exist to call them;
    -   what **parameters** (inputs) each function accepts;
    -   what kind of data it **returns**.

### Start with the Package Website

-   Every SportsDataVerse package has a documentation website.
-   It gives you the big picture: what data is available, what functions to use, and how to get started.
-   For hoopR: <https://hoopr.sportsdataverse.org/articles/getting-started-hoopR.html>

### Then Check Individual Functions in R

-   Once you know which function you want, use `?` in the RStudio Console:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| filename: "R console"

?load_nba_team_box
```

-   The help page shows the **Usage**, **Arguments** and **Value** sections.
-   **Arguments** = the parameters you can set when calling the function.
-   **Value** = what the function gives back (usually a data frame).

### What You Learn from the Docs

-   `load_nba_team_box()` has one key parameter: `seasons`.
-   `seasons` accepts a **numeric vector** — you can request one season or several.
-   The function returns a data frame with **one row per team per game**.
-   Knowing this before you run the code saves trial and error.

### The Same Pattern, Different Sport

-   For football, the `worldfootballR` package works the same way.
-   Start with the Transfermarkt documentation: <https://jaseziv.github.io/worldfootballR/articles/extract-transfermarkt-data.html>
-   Then check individual functions in R with `?tm_player_market_values`.
-   You would discover parameters like `country_name`, `start_year` and `league_url`.
-   The documentation tells you which values are valid for each parameter.

### General Workflow

```{=html}
<div style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; gap:0.2em; margin-top:0.8em; font-size:0.95em;">

  <div style="padding:0.4em 1.5em; background:#264653; color:white; border-radius:8px; text-align:center; width:80%;">
    1. Browse the <strong>package website</strong> — what data &amp; functions are available?
  </div>

  <div style="font-size:1.3em; color:#888;">↓</div>

  <div style="padding:0.4em 1.5em; background:#2a9d8f; color:white; border-radius:8px; text-align:center; width:80%;">
    2. Check <code style="background:rgba(255,255,255,0.2); padding:0.1em 0.3em; border-radius:3px;">?function_name</code> in R — read the <strong>Arguments</strong>
  </div>

  <div style="font-size:1.3em; color:#888;">↓</div>

  <div style="padding:0.4em 1.5em; background:#e76f51; color:white; border-radius:8px; text-align:center; width:80%;">
    3. Choose your parameter values (e.g. season, league)
  </div>

  <div style="font-size:1.3em; color:#888;">↓</div>

  <div style="padding:0.4em 1.5em; background:#EF426F; color:white; border-radius:8px; text-align:center; width:80%;">
    4. Call the function and inspect the result
  </div>

</div>
```

## Good Practice in Data-Sourcing

### Pull → Inspect → Summarise → Save

-   This is the core data-sourcing habit for the module.
-   It applies to **any** sport and **any** data package.

### Step 1: Pull

-   Load your packages and call the data-sourcing function.
-   Use the parameters you identified from the documentation.
-   The result is a **data frame** — typically one row per event, match, or game.

### Step 2: Inspect

-   Before doing anything else, check what you received.

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| filename: "R code"

dim(data)       # how many rows and columns?
names(data)     # what are the column names?
glimpse(data)   # what types and values does each column have?
```

-   You can also click the data frame in the **Environment** panel to browse it visually.
-   **Never** summarise before you have inspected.

### Step 3: Summarise

-   Filter the data to the subset you need (e.g. regular season, a specific league).
-   Use `group_by()` to split by team, player, driver, etc.
-   Use `summarise()` to collapse each group into one row of aggregated statistics.
-   Use `mutate()` to create new columns from the summary (e.g. win percentage).
-   Choose the variables that matter for **your** sport and **your** analysis.

### Step 4: Save

-   Save your prepared data so you do not need to re-run API calls every time.
-   Keep downloaded API returns in `data/raw/`.
-   Keep cleaned, reusable tables in `data/cleaned/`.
-   `.rds` preserves R objects exactly (column types, factor levels, everything).
-   `.csv` is universal and can be opened in Excel, Python, or any other tool.
-   Use both: `.rds` for your own workflow, `.csv` for sharing.

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| filename: "R code"

saveRDS(raw_data, "data/raw/my_raw_data.rds")
write_csv(raw_data, "data/raw/my_raw_data.csv")

saveRDS(my_data, "data/cleaned/my_prepared_data.rds")
write_csv(my_data, "data/cleaned/my_prepared_data.csv")
```

## Applying This Workflow

### The Pattern Is Transferable

-   The specific functions and column names change from sport to sport.
-   The **workflow** does not:
    1.  Read the documentation.
    2.  Choose your parameters.
    3.  Pull → Inspect → Summarise → Save.
-   For your assignment, you will do exactly this with a sport of your choosing.

## Version Control

### Save and Publish Your Work

-   After each meaningful piece of work, commit and push.
-   Use `_publish.sh` to do it in one step:

```{bash}
#| eval: false
#| filename: "RStudio Terminal"

./_publish.sh "Your commit message here"
```

-   Write a commit message that describes **what** you did, not just "update".

## Homework

### Go To Workbook...

![](/images/workbook-hw.png){style="box-shadow: 5px 5px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.5);"}

### Questions?

```{=html}
<div style="position:relative; height:460px;">
  <div style="position:absolute; top:58%; left:50%; transform:translate(-50%, -50%); text-align:center; width:70%;">
    <p style="font-size:1.2em; margin-bottom:0.5em;"><strong>Shunya Kimura</strong></p>
    <p style="font-size:1em; margin-bottom:0;">s.kimura@liverpool.ac.uk</p>
  </div>
  <div style="position:absolute; right:0; bottom:-42px; line-height:0;">
    <img src="../assets/ULMS744-logo.svg" alt="ULMS744 logo" style="width:220px; max-width:100%;">
  </div>
</div>
```
